subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are communicated both clearly and precisely with appropriate labels and uncertainties.
Data processing is carried out appropriately and supports the conclusion (averaging, linearisation, regression).
Uncertainties are considered and propagated, with error bars and slope uncertainty included.
weaknesses: Raw data uses negative velocities leading to potential signâconvention confusion.
Errorâpropagation was executed with relative sums instead of root-sum-square, introducing inaccuracies.
Viscosity calculation contained a unit-conversion error affecting the magnitude by orders of magnitude.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication and processing meet the 5â6 band descriptors, but substantive calculation and propagation errors (unit conversion, errorâpropagation method) prevent a flawless topâband performance. A score of 5 reflects strong analysis with significant but fixable omissions.

strengths: Research question is embedded in a specific, purposeful context with a clear literature gap.
Methodology is described in full detail (variable table, stepâbyâstep procedure, uncertainties) allowing reproducibility.
Controls and methodological considerations are thoroughly explained, showing clear links to the research question.
weaknesses: Did not define the Reynolds number regime to justify model selection explicitly.
Minor formatting inconsistencies in citations and equation annotation.
Camera setup schematic and interval ambient temperature recordings were suggested but not included.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the 5â6 descriptor: the question is in a specific context, methods are fully reproducible, and methodological considerations are explained. Suggestions are minor enhancements rather than substantive omissions, meriting the top mark of 6.

strengths: Conclusion is fully consistent with the data, directly answering the research question with quantitative support.
Comparison to both Stokesâ law and quadratic drag models is justified in the context of accepted scientific values.
Logical flow from analysis to interpretation is strong, showing deep understanding of the physics.
weaknesses: Did not mention assumptions about drag coefficients which would aid in generalizing results.
Could briefly address the effect of dragâcoefficient variability on broader applications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands fulfill the 5â6 descriptors: the conclusion is justified and fully consistent with the analysis, and the scientific context comparison is well supported. Minor suggestions do not detract from a topâband performance, justifying a score of 6.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified and their impact on terminal velocity trends is discussed quantitatively.
Suggested improvements such as using intact wrapper stacks are realistic and directly linked to reducing systematic error.
weaknesses: Omitted discussion of ambient temperature monitoring to validate viscosity assumptions.
Did not propose measurement or estimation of drag coefficients to account for shapeâdependent effects.
Reynolds number calculation was recommended but not included as an improvement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: One strand meets the 5â6 band, but the other only reaches the 3â4 band due to limited scope of improvements. The mixed performance warrants a top mark of 4 for Evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated and the introduction embeds it in a focused discussion of drag, Stokesâ law and the absence of data for muffin-wrapper geometries.  Background theory is used to motivate why these wrappers are of interest and to outline two rival physical models.  This establishes a **specific, relevant and purposeful context** rather than a broad or generic one, fully satisfying the 5-6 descriptor.

Instrument precision is identified, propagated uncertainties are calculated for average velocities, error bars are plotted, and slope uncertainties are obtained with maxâmin line method.  While environmental temperature is assumed constant, the treatment is still **appropriate and largely complete**, matching the 5-6 band.

The student **explains why each control is required** (e.g., radius affects Stokes and drag equations; drop height long enough for terminal velocity; camera geometry and ambient temperature).  Relationships between these factors and terminal velocity are articulated, showing an understanding of how sufficient and relevant data will be secured.  This explanatory depth meets the 5-6 level.

Specific weaknessesâwrapper separation altering radius, unknown drag coefficient variation, parallaxâare not only identified but **their quantitative impact on terminal velocity trends is discussed**.  This explanatory approach aligns with the 5-6 descriptor.

A detailed variable table, step-by-step procedure, photographs and equipment uncertainties allow another student to replicate the work with minimal interpretation.  Key features such as camera position, scale calibration, number of wrappers and trials are all specified quantitatively.  Only minor formatting issues remain, so the description is essentially **fully reproducible**, meriting the top band.

Data are averaged correctly, square-root transformations are applied, linear regression is used, and viscosity is calculated from the slope.  No major computational errors are apparent and the analysis supports later conclusions.  Thus processing is **accurate and appropriate**, warranting a score in the top band.

Improvements (using intact wrapper stacks, maintaining uniform shape) are relevant and realistic, and the student explains how these would reduce systematic error.  However, suggestions are limited to shape uniformity; other identified issues (camera alignment, environmental monitoring) are not fully developed.  Hence the work best fits the 3-4 band, warranting a top-of-band score of 4.

Raw and processed data are presented in well-structured tables with units, significant figures and stated uncertainties; graphs include titles, labelled axes, error bars and best-fit/limit lines.  Minor typographical slips do not impede understanding.  Hence communication is **both clear and precise**, fulfilling the highest band descriptor.

The student rigorously contrasts experimental findings with Stokesâ law and accepted air viscosity, then with the quadratic drag model, explaining why one model is inappropriate.  This is a **relevant and well-justified comparison to established scientific context**, fitting the 5-6 band.

The conclusion directly answers the research question and is fully backed by quantitative evidence (graph correlation coefficients, slope comparison, viscosity mismatch).  It demonstrates logical consistency with the analysed data, satisfying the highest descriptor.

annotations.comment: The research question is stated clearly and situated within a specific context highlighting the gap in drag data for muffinâwrapper geometries.
Add units to the maximum and minimum velocity columns to ensure complete precision in the raw data table.
Qualitative observation of wrapper separation is insightful; quantify the separation angle or gap to integrate into uncertainty analysis.
Errorâpropagation in the sample calculations sums relative uncertainties incorrectly; use rootâsumâsquare or standard propagation rules for more accuracy.
Show units explicitly in each step of the errorâpropagation formulas to confirm dimensional consistency.
The processed data table is wellâstructured, showing clear transformations and uncertainty values for linearisation.
Include the coefficient of determination (RÂ²) alongside the slope uncertainty in Figure 5 to quantify goodness of fit.
The viscosity calculation yields a value orders of magnitude off due to unitâconversion errors; rework the algebra including the 10â»â¶ factor correctly.
Revisit the conversion from slope units to viscosity, explicitly including the 10â»â¶ PaÂ·s factor to avoid large systematic deviations.
The introduction uses relevant background but would be strengthened by defining the Reynolds number regime to justify use of Stokesâ law versus the quadratic drag model.
When presenting Stokesâ law (Equation 1), include the valid velocity range and a citation format consistent with IB guidelines.
Excellent identification of the literature gap regarding drag behaviour of nonâstandard shapes, focusing the investigation on muffin wrappers.
The hypothesis is well connected to the theoretical models, clearly predicting a squareâroot relationship for terminal velocity.
Rearrange and annotate Equation 1 more explicitly, ensuring units are consistent when calculating viscosity from slope.
The variable table is comprehensive with detailed measurement methods and uncertainties, supporting reproducibility.
The table header row is duplicated; remove the extra "Variables / Explanation" header to improve clarity.
Include a schematic or dimensions of the camera setup in the methodology to further minimize parallax uncertainty.
Record ambient temperature at set intervals rather than assuming constancy, to substantiate viscosity stability.
Raw data table uses negative velocity values which may confuse sign conventions; consider using absolute values directly in the table.
The conclusion effectively answers the research question, linking results back to the proposed dragâforce model.
Consider adding a brief mention in the conclusion of dragâcoefficient assumptions and their impact on generalizing the results.
Identification of shape separation as a source of systematic error is well explained with its impact on terminal velocity behavior.
The linearisation in Figure 6 is presented clearly, with slope, uncertainty and correlation coefficient indicating strong fit.
Propose measuring or estimating the drag coefficient (e.g., via windâtunnel or literature values) to account for shapeâdependent variations.
Include a calculation of Reynolds number to confirm that flow regime aligns with assumptions of Stokesâ law or quadratic drag.
The evaluation omits potential effects of ambient temperature fluctuations; recommend periodic logging to validate viscosity assumptions.
Reference formatting could be more consistent: include DOIs or complete URLs and standardize access dates.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
